Lithuania is officially known as the Republic of Lithuania, a country located in Europe.
It shares borders with Latvia to the north, Belarus to the east and south, Poland to the south, and has a maritime border with Russia to the southwest.
Lithuania covers an area of 65,300 km² (25,200 sq mi) and has a population estimate of approximately 2.9 million as of 2025.
The capital and largest city of Lithuania is Vilnius, with other major cities including Kaunas and Klaipėda.
Lithuania has a medium inequality index, a high standard of living, and uses the Euro as its currency. Its official language is Lithuanian.
Lithuania was unified in the 1230s by a ruler who formed the kingdom on July 6, 1253.
By the 14th century, the Grand Duchy of Lithuania became the largest country in Europe and entered a union with Poland in 1386.
The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth was established in 1569 and lasted over two centuries until its partition between 1772 and 1795.
Lithuania declared independence in 1918, was occupied during WWII, and became the first Soviet republic to break away in 1990.
Lithuania is a modern republic and a member of several international organizations, including the European Union and NATO.
Lithuania has a history dating back to approximately 10,000 years ago with initial settlements by traveling hunters.
Inhabited by various Baltic tribes, the territory developed a unique cultural and linguistic identity due to geographic isolation.
The first written reference to Lithuania appeared in 1009 AD, marking the beginning of recorded history for the state.
In the 13th century, Mindaugas unified various tribes to establish the State of Lithuania and was crowned king in 1253.
The Grand Duchy of Lithuania faced invasions from the Teutonic Knights and was characterized by continued military efforts and diverse cultural practices.
Late 13th century: Lithuanian dynasty rises to power, significantly expanding territory to ~650,000 km2 by the 14th century.
Formation of alliance with Poland in 1385 through the Union of Krewo; both countries ruled by the Gediminids dynasty.
Decisive victory in the Battle of Grunwald (1410) ends German attacks; Treaty of Thorn (1422) concludes conflicts with Teutonic Knights.
Union with Poland formalized in 1569, creating the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, with distinct governance structures for Lithuania.
Mid-17th century saw territorial losses during the Deluge; Lithuanian capital Vilnius occupied but regained by 1661 after revolts.
The Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth was partitioned three times by neighboring countries, leading to its dissolution in 1795 after the failed Vilnius uprising.
Following the annexation, Lithuania became part of a new administrative region under Russian rule, which enforced policies of Russification.
Efforts to restore Lithuanian and Polish statehood included the November Uprising (1830-1831) and the January Uprising (1863-1864), both of which ended in Russian victories and increased repression.
The intellectual revival in Lithuania was spearheaded by figures like Simonas Daukantas, who aimed to renew native culture and historical awareness with works on Lithuania's past.

There was an emphasis on the historical significance of Lithuania prior to its union with Poland in 1569, with scholars exploring cultural and linguistic connections to highlight Lithuania's heritage.
Lithuania resisted Russification through secret publishing and homeschooling.
A 1905 congress led to demands for wider autonomy for Lithuania.
In 1918, Lithuania proclaimed independence, later becoming a short-lived kingdom.
Lithuania faced occupations from Poland and the Soviet Union during the 20th century.
A renewed independence movement emerged in the late 1980s, culminating in a major event in 1989.
Lithuania restored its independence on 11 March 1990, becoming the first Soviet-occupied state to do so.
The Soviet Union imposed an economic blockade on Lithuania, cutting off supplies and essentials, which lasted for 74 days without Lithuania renouncing its independence.
In January 1991, a coup attempt by Soviet forces led to significant public support for Lithuanian independence, resulting in casualties and injuries.
Lithuania officially adopted a new constitution on 25 October 1992 and elected its first president on 14 February 1993, marking a stable political transition.
Lithuania joined various international organizations including the WTO in 2001, NATO in 2004, and adopted the Euro in 2015, highlighting its integration into Western institutions.
Lithuania is located in the Baltic region, covering an area of 65,300 km².
It has a sandy coastline of 99 km, with only 38 km facing the open sea, mainly sheltered by the Curonian sand peninsula.
Lithuania features a diverse landscape smoothed by glaciers, with moderate lowlands and highlands, and its highest point is 294 meters.
The climate is temperate, with both maritime and continental influences, leading to cold winters and mild summers.
Average winter temperatures can drop to -20°C, with extremes reaching -34°C in coastal areas and -43°C in the east.
Average annual precipitation: 800 mm on the coast, 900 mm in Samogitia, 600 mm in the east; snow from October to April.
Growing season: 202 days in the west, 169 days in the east; severe storms common in coastal areas.
Temperature records show warm periods in the late 18th century, a cool 19th century, and warming trends from the 1960s onward.
Lithuania adopted the Environmental Protection Act in 1992 to regulate biodiversity preservation and aims to cut emissions significantly by 2030.
Lithuania features abundant lakes (over 3,000), dense forests, and notable rivers, with a landscape that supports rich biodiversity.
Forests cover one-third of Lithuania's territory and contribute 11% to industrial production.
Lithuanian ecosystems include forests, wetlands, meadows, and urban areas, with significant loss of wetlands (70%) due to drainage.
Agricultural land constitutes 54% of territory; 400,000 ha is unused, fostering invasive species.
Wildlife populations are recovering; Lithuania has about 250,000 larger wild animals, with a notable increase in forests.
Key species include deer (120,000), wild boar (55,000), and predators like wolves (27,000), with endangered species present.
Lithuania restored its independence on 11 March 1990 and has maintained strong democratic traditions since then.
The first independent general elections took place on 25 October 1992 with public debates about the constitution and presidency.
The president of Lithuania is directly elected for a five-year term, with a maximum of two terms, overseeing foreign affairs and national security.
The Lithuanian parliament, the Seimas, consists of 141 members elected for four years, requiring a minimum of 5% of the national vote for party representation.
Lithuania was one of the first countries to grant women the right to vote in 1919, ahead of many other democratic nations.
Lithuania has a fragmented multi-party system with common coalition governments.
Elections for parliament occur every four years on the second Sunday of October, with specific eligibility requirements for candidates.
The President serves a five-year term, must be at least 40 years old, and can serve no more than two terms.
Municipal councils in Lithuania are elected every four years, with council sizes depending on municipality population.
In the 2023 elections, the newly elected council members included 1,498 members and a political party that won 358 municipal council seats and 17 mayors.
Lithuania has 11 allocated seats in the EU parliament as of 2024, with elections held on a Sunday for all citizens over 18.
Candidates must be at least 21 years old, citizens of Lithuania or the EU residing in Lithuania, and cannot stand for election in multiple countries.
Eligibility to run is restricted for certain groups, including those serving a court sentence, military servicemen, and certain officials.
Lithuania's legal history includes the compilation of its first laws in 1468 and several statutes adopted in the 16th century.
The current legal framework was established in 1992 following independence, emphasizing formalism and rationalization in law.
Lithuania's legal system became integral on 1 May 2004 after gaining independence from the Soviet Union.
Crime in Lithuania has been declining, with 63,846 crimes reported in 2017, including a notable reduction in serious crimes such as homicides and serious bodily harm.
The Lithuanian Police, along with specialized units, are primarily responsible for law enforcement in the country.
Corruption remains a significant concern, with 95% of Lithuanians perceiving it as widespread, although levels are reported to be decreasing over the past decade.
Lithuania has the highest incarceration rate in the EU, attributed not to a high crime rate but to high repression and low trust in the justice system.
Lithuania's administrative divisions were established in 1994 and modified in 2000 for EU compliance.
Administrative units in Lithuania include counties (*apskritys*), municipalities (*savivaldybės*), and elderships (*seniūnijos*).
Municipalities are the primary administrative units, with councils elected every four years and mayors directly elected since 2015.
Elderships serve as the smallest administrative units, primarily responsible for local public services and welfare distribution.
Some citizens feel elderships lack real power and visibility, limiting their effectiveness in addressing local issues.
Lithuania became a UN member on 18 September 1991, participating in various international agreements and organizations.
It established diplomatic relations with 149 countries and became a member of NATO in 2001 and the Eurozone in 2018.
Lithuania hosted the 2011 Ministerial Council Meeting and held the presidency of the Council of the EU in the second half of 2013.
It cooperates with Nordic and Baltic states through various formats like NB6, focusing on EU foreign affairs.
The Council of Baltic Sea States (CBSS) was formed in 1992 to promote regional integration and cooperation among its members.
Lithuania is part of the NORDPLUS programme with Nordic and Baltic countries, emphasizing educational collaboration.
The Baltic Development Forum (BDF) is a nonprofit organization aimed at enhancing regional cooperation among businesses in the Baltic Sea area; its 12th summit occurred in Vilnius in 2010.
Lithuania has significant diplomatic tensions with Poland regarding the treatment of the Polish minority, while maintaining strong support for Georgia's EU and NATO integration.
Lithuania was elected to the UN Security Council in 2013, becoming the first Baltic state to do so, and actively condemned Russia during the War in Donbas.
In 2021, Lithuania faced diplomatic tensions with China which halted imports from Lithuania due to its stance on Taiwan, amid increased Chinese espionage activities against Lithuania.
The 2023 event took place in the capital of Lithuania.
The Lithuanian Armed Forces comprise 20,000 active personnel and include multiple commands like Logistics and Military Police.
Compulsory military service was reintroduced in 2015, ending a previous suspension since 2008.
Lithuania is a NATO member since 2004 and participates in international missions, including training Ukrainian soldiers.
The National Defence Policy aims to protect the state’s territorial integrity and participate in NATO and EU missions.
Lithuania allocated over 2% of its GDP to national defense in 2019, after previously lagging behind NATO spending guidelines.
Lithuanian President called for increased NATO troop deployments in Eastern Europe on April 22, 2022.
The Lithuanian economy is classified as open and mixed, with services (67% of GDP), industry (29%), and agriculture (3%) as its main sectors.
Lithuania adopted the euro as its national currency on January 1, 2015, replacing the Lithuanian litas.
Exports accounted for 81% of GDP in 2017, with significant trade partnerships with countries like Russia, Latvia, and Poland.
Over 95% of foreign direct investment (FDI) in Lithuania comes from EU countries, with Sweden being the largest investor.
In 2017, FDI in Lithuania peaked with record greenfield investment projects; major contributors included the US, Germany, and the UK.
From 2004 to 2016, significant emigration occurred in Lithuania, impacting labor market dynamics and causing salaries to outpace labor efficiency.
As of 2022, the average wealth per adult in Lithuania was $32,000, with a gross monthly salary of €2,000 in 2023 Q2.
Lithuania has become a hub for e-money and cryptocurrency, with notable government support for fintech innovation and a high number of issued licenses.
Agriculture in Lithuania has a long history, dating back to 3000-1000 BC, and has been a key occupation.
Lithuania joined the EU in 2004, leading to reforms in agricultural standards focused on food safety and purity.
In 2016, agricultural production in Lithuania was valued at € billion, with significant exports amounting to €4,385 million, accounting for 19% of total exports.
Organic farming is gaining traction, with 2,539 certified organic farms covering 225,542 hectares, primarily growing cereals and grasses.
The establishment of Vilnius University in 1579 fostered scientific development in Lithuania, leading to contributions from notable scientists in various fields.
World wars negatively impacted Lithuanian science and academia.
Lithuanian scholars found success abroad in various fields including mathematics and philosophy.
Major advances in high-tech sectors include contributions from companies like Šviesos konversija and the Laser Research Center.
Lithuania launched three satellites and has multiple R&D institutions involved in global scientific efforts.
Since 2008, initiatives have been launched to enhance scientific research infrastructure and foster business-science collaboration through R&D Valleys.
Lithuania ranks 15th among EU countries and was 35th globally in tourism in 2024.
In 2023, 1 million foreign tourists visited Lithuania, with the highest numbers from neighboring countries.
Domestic tourism is increasing with up to 1000 attractions, especially in major cities and seaside resorts.
Lithuania has a highly developed communication infrastructure, with 97% LTE coverage and increasing mobile broadband speeds.
A rural broadband project aimed to enhance internet access, projecting 77% household access by 2021.
Lithuania had the highest FTTH penetration rate in Europe as of September 2016.
The country has a railway network of 1,762 km, most of which is incompatible with European standards.
Rail transport is crucial in Lithuania, with over half of inland freight transported by rail.
Transportation is the third largest sector in Lithuania's economy, with significant international truck traffic.
The Lithuanian Inland Waterways Authority is reviving cargo shipping on the Nemunas, which is expected to reduce truck trips significantly.
Vilnius International Airport is the largest in Lithuania and the 91st busiest in Europe, serving millions of passengers.
Lithuania's energy strategy focuses on systematic diversification of energy imports, with significant investments forecasted for energy independence initiatives.
Lithuania transitioned from being an electricity exporter to an importer after the closure of its nuclear power plants in 2004 and 2009.
The Klaipėda LNG terminal was opened in 2014 to reduce reliance on natural gas monopolies, providing full coverage of Lithuania's demand.
Electric interconnections with Sweden and Poland were completed in 2015 and 2022, aiding energy diversification and stability in the Baltic region.
Lithuania's population has remained genetically homogeneous since the Neolithic period, with no significant genetic differences among ethnic subgroups.
As of 2021, Lithuania's age structure includes: 0-14 years (417,230); 15-64 years (1,830,867); and 65+ years (560,283).
The median age in Lithuania was 44 years in 2022, with notable differences between genders (41 for males, 47 for females).
Lithuania has a total fertility rate (TFR) of children born per woman in 2021, with increasing mean ages for childbirth and first childbirth.
Ethnic Lithuanians constitute about five-sixths of the population, with substantial minorities including Poles, Russians, Belarusians, and Ukrainians.
The largest minority in Lithuania is Polish, concentrated in southeastern regions and constituting a majority in some areas.
Lithuania has a long history of diverse communities, including around 2,250 Tatars and 196 Karaites as of 2021.
The official language is Lithuanian, but minority languages like Polish, Russian, and others are significant, especially in areas like Šalčininkai and Vilnius.
About 80% of young people in Lithuania are proficient in English, followed by smaller percentages for other languages like German and Polish.
Urbanization has been ongoing since the 1990s, with two-thirds of the population living in urban areas by the early 21st century, and Vilnius is highly ranked in European city standings.
Lithuania has a mix of state-funded and private healthcare, providing free services to citizens and long-term residents.
Healthcare reforms from 2003-2012 included the expansion of ambulatory services and primary care in hospitals.
In 2016, Lithuania ranked 27th in Europe for healthcare systems based on various indicators, improving to 19th in 2024.
The country has a high suicide rate, which has decreased since the 1990s but remains the highest in the EU, influenced by economic and social factors.
Most healthcare institutions in Lithuania became non-profit by 2000, with the Ministry of Health overseeing public health services and hospitals.
Lithuania has 5 Territorial Health Insurance Funds, covering major cities, with a 9% contribution from economically active individuals.
Emergency medical services are free for all residents, and secondary care requires a referral.
Catholicism is the main religion in Lithuania, officially recognized since 1387 and has faced historical persecution.
The population includes a significant Russian minority practicing Orthodox Christianity and has a small, growing Hindu community.
Lithuania's Jewish community, once large and influential, has drastically declined due to historical events, with only about 4,000 remaining post-WWII.
Romuva is a revived pagan faith in Lithuania, emphasizing nature sanctity and rooted in historical traditions.
The number of Baltic faith adherents in Lithuania increased from 1,270 to 5,118 between earlier records and the 2011 census.
Lithuania mandates a ten-year education system that ensures free higher education for eligible students, overseen by the Ministry of Education and Science.
Despite a 100% literacy rate and high school attendance above the EU average, Lithuanian education faces funding issues and low teacher salaries.

Lithuania ranks among the top countries in the world for tertiary education attainment but has seen a significant decrease in its youth population, affecting teacher-student ratios and school operations.

As of 2008, Lithuania had 15 public and 6 private universities, and 16 public and 11 private colleges, with a government decision to reduce the number of universities due to declining high-school enrollment.
The largest university in Lithuania and the largest technical university in the Baltic States were mentioned, but not named in the text.
Lithuanian is the official state language, with about 3 million native speakers in Lithuania and 2 million abroad.
Lithuanian is a Baltic language closely related to Latvian and retains many archaic features, making it significant for linguistic studies.
There are two main dialects of Lithuanian: Aukštaitian and Samogitian, with distinct pronunciations and vocabulary, leading some linguists to consider Samogitian a separate language.
The first grammar book in Lithuanian, *Grammatica Litvanica*, was published in Latin in 1653 by Daniel Klein, marking a shift to a standard Lithuanian language.
Klein collected linguistic materials that were published in 20 volumes, which remain important for text editing and research.
Early Lithuanian literature included works written in Latin, with notable examples from the Lithuanian King and writings from around the Middle Ages.
Pioneering authors like the anonymous author of *Carmen de statura* and Petrus Roysius made contributions to Lithuanian literature and culture during the 16th century.

17th-century Lithuanian scholars continued to write in Latin, including works on history, theology, and rhetoric, with the first printed history (*Historia Lithuania*) also being produced during this time.
First printed Lithuanian book *Katekizmo prasti žodžiai* was published in 1547, marking the beginning of Lithuanian literature.
16th and 17th-century Lithuanian literature was primarily religious.
Donelaitis' poem *Metai* is a landmark work in Lithuanian fiction literature.
The Book Smugglers movement helped preserve Lithuanian language and literature during the 19th-century Tsarist annexation.
20th and 21st-century Lithuanian literature features notable authors like , Renata Šerelytė, and Valdas Papievis.
The largest art museum in Lithuania was founded in 1933, along with notable institutions focusing on Lithuanian archaeology and modern art.
Key figures in Lithuanian art include composer M.K. Čiurlionis and notable painters from the 18th and 19th centuries.
Lithuania has a vibrant theatre scene with several prominent theatres and well-known directors and actors, participating in various festivals.
Lithuanian cinema began in the late 19th century, producing significant films despite challenges during occupation, with a revival post-independence.
The oldest operational cinema in Lithuania dates back to 1940, originally used for propaganda during the occupation.
4,265,414 cinema tickets sold in Lithuania in 2018.
Lithuanian folk music features stringed and wind instruments, often used for rituals.
Key genres include Multipart Songs, Wedding Songs, and Work Songs.
Mikalojus Constantin Čiurlionis, a notable Lithuanian composer and painter, influenced modern Lithuanian culture and is a pioneer of abstract art.
The Dainų šventė (Song Festival) has been held every four years since 1990, gathering around 30,000 participants.
The Vilnius Jazz School was established in 1970-71 by the Ganelin/Tarasov/Chekasin trio.
During the Soviet occupation, Lithuanian rock bands began emerging around 1965, expressing dissent through lyrics and metaphors.
The band 'Antis' used satire in their music to criticize the Soviet regime, notably in their song 'Zombiai.'
Foje was a popular band in the early years of independence, with their vocalist becoming a prominent figure in Lithuanian music and charity events.
Lithuanian cuisine features local products and dairy, influenced by the region's cool climate and agricultural practices.
Dairy products like cottage cheese, sour cream, and butter are staples in Lithuanian cuisine.
Traditional meat products include smoked sausages, lard, and smoked ham.
Soups such as boletus and cold-beet soup, along with various porridge types, are common in the diet.
Rye bread is a daily staple and has cultural significance in rituals and ceremonies.
Lithuania has a thriving beer culture, with high per capita consumption and a growing microbrewery scene.
Eight Lithuanian restaurants are featured in the White Guide Baltic Top 30, with local rankings to be announced on June 13, 2024.
Lithuania's constitution allows freedom of expression but restricts hate speech, disinformation, and denial of historical crimes.
In 2021, top selling daily newspapers included 'Lietuvos Rytas' and 'Kauno Diena', while popular weekly newspapers featured 'Savaitė' and 'Žmonės'.
The most popular television stations in Lithuania were 'LNK', 'TV3', and 'BTV', with radio station 'M-1' leading in daily listeners.
Lithuania celebrates significant public holidays, including Statehood Day on July 6 and Independence Day on February 16, totaling 13 public holidays as of 2018.
An annual festival commemorates an important historical figure's death and attracts thousands of visitors and craftsmen.
Key national holidays in Lithuania include New Year's Day, Independence Day, and Statehood Day, each with cultural significance.
Lithuania celebrates various traditional festivals, including Easter and pagan traditions associated with summer and autumn.
Basketball is the most popular sport in Lithuania, with a rich history of success in international competitions.
The country has produced notable NBA players and hosted major basketball events like the Eurobasket.
Lithuania has won a total of Olympic medals, including 6 gold medals primarily in swimming.
The most successful athlete of independent Lithuania has achieved success in multiple Olympic Games, winning gold and bronze medals.
A notable gold medal was won by a 15-year-old swimmer in London, which increased the sport's popularity in Lithuania.
Lithuania hosted its first FIFA tournament, showcasing its capability to host international sports events.
Few Lithuanian athletes have had notable success in winter sports despite having facilities for training.
